title: Enhanced mechanical properties of a Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel by increasing hot-rolling reduction
abstract_summary: The study investigates the effect of hot-rolling reduction (45%, 68%, 82%) on the microstructure and mechanical properties of a Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel. Increased reduction refined grains and raised dislocation density, raising yield strength to 648 MPa and ultimate tensile strength to 976 MPa with 50% total elongation and 105 J/m² toughness. The strengthening is attributed to grain boundary and dislocation contributions, while the minor decrease in ductility arises from faster saturation of slip band refinement and earlier microband formation due to finer grains.
research_objective: To clarify how increasing hot-rolling reduction influences the microstructure evolution and mechanical properties of Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel, and to systematically elucidate the strengthening and strain-hardening mechanisms for achieving an optimized strength–ductility–toughness combination.
main_process_variables: Cooling after rolling: water quenching to room temperature

sample_aliases.meaning: hot-rolled plate with 45% thickness reduction (final thickness 60 mm)
hot-rolled plate with 68% thickness reduction (final thickness 35 mm)
hot-rolled plate with 82% thickness reduction (final thickness 20 mm)

key_sections: Introduction: background on low-density steels, limitations of age-hardening κ-carbides, motivation for using hot-rolling grain refinement, and study objective.
Experimental methods: details of material preparation, hot-rolling process, SEM/EBSD characterization, and mechanical property testing.
Results: examination of microstructure (grain size, dislocation density, precipitates) and mechanical properties (tensile curves, strength, ductility, toughness) at different reductions.

Discussion: quantitative analysis of grain boundary and dislocation strengthening contributions, interpretation of strain-hardening stages and deformation substructure evolution (slip bands, microbands), and rationalization of ductility trends.
Conclusions: summary of strength–ductility–toughness combination achieved and key mechanistic findings.
